pattern: An arithmetic progression is fully determined by its smallest value and its common step, so sorting exposes both immediately -- checking that every consecutive gap matches the first one confirms a valid reordering exists.
hints: Reordering into an arithmetic progression is possible exactly when there's a way to line the numbers up so consecutive differences are constant -- sorting is a safe way to try, since any valid progression is itself either sorted ascending or descending.
Sort the values, then look at the very first gap between two neighbours -- if a progression exists at all, that gap has to be the common difference for the whole sequence.
Walk the sorted array once and compare every consecutive gap to that first gap; if any pair disagrees, no reordering works, otherwise every one of them will match.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, reorders to a valid progression
minimum length, two elements always form a progression
two equal elements, zero common difference
duplicates that break a would-be progression
duplicate pair combined with a gap that fails divisibility

stress.description: mix of shuffled valid progressions and pure random arrays near max length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    if rng.random() < 0.5:
        start = rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000)
        diff = rng.randint(-2000, 2000)
        arr = [start + i * diff for i in range(n)]
        arr = [max(-1000000, min(1000000, v)) for v in arr]
        rng.shuffle(arr)
    else:
        arr = [rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [arr]
